"Beware that, when fighting monsters, you yourself do not become a monster.... for when you gaze long into the abyss, the abyss gazes also into you." 

Those words from the philosopher Nietzsche are not about ghosts or demons. They are about a danger each of us can recognize. It is about the peril of the fight. When we confront a great darkness, such as injustice or cruelty and suffering, we risk being consumed by it. We can become the very thing we sought to defeat.

Consider the story of Harry Roque. Here was a man who once stood with the victims. As a human rights lawyer, he gazed into the abyss. He looked directly at the suffering of the powerless and the ravages of impunity.

He gave voice to the victims of massacre. He comforted women seeking justice. He knew the language of international law. He saw the ICC as a tool for righteousness, a light to illuminate darkness.

But then he joined the administration of Rodrigo Duterte. He became the public voice for a war on drugs that created thousands more victims. The advocate who once used his voice to condemn injustice then used it to explain injustice away. He did not just gaze into the abyss. He stepped into its service.

Now history turns. Duterte is to be brought before the very court Roque once lauded. The light of the ICC is finally aimed at the darkness.

So we must ask what this moment demands of Harry Roque. 

The easy verdict is to say he is a hypocrite, a sellout. But the harder and more important question is this: What do we owe to principles after we have betrayed them?

That question is whether someone who justified the abyss can rediscover the moral clarity that once condemned it. The light of justice can expose the facts, but only an examination of one's own conscience can illuminate the darkness within.

The court will judge Duterte. But for Roque, for the supporters of Duterte, and for all of us watching, the harder judgment is the one we make upon ourselves. It is the judgment we face when we finally look away from the abyss and ask ourselves what we have become.

Can you turn away from the abyss once it has taken up residence in your soul?
